content.text: that exposes 300+ models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, Mistral, and others behind a single OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. You manage one account and one API key; OpenRouter handles provider authentication, rate limit pooling, and smart routing.

attrs.alt: OpenRouter architecture — your app sends requests to openrouter.ai/api/v1, which routes to 300+ models with smart fallbacks and cost routing

content.text: , add credits, and generate an API key. No provider-specific accounts or keys are required.
pip install openai   # use the standard OpenAI Python SDK

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1',
    api_key='sk-or-...',   # your OpenRouter API key
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='anthropic/claude-sonnet-5',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Explain backpropagation in two sentences.'}],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
The only change from a regular OpenAI call is:
and an OpenRouter key. Every model on the platform is identified as
Changing the model string is the only code change needed to try a different provider or model family.

# Switch provider by changing the model string — no other code changes needed
models = [
    'openai/gpt-4o-mini',
    'anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5',
    'meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct',
    'google/gemini-2.0-flash-001',
    'mistralai/mistral-small',
]

for model in models:
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Hello!'}],
    )
    print(f'{model}: {resp.choices[0].message.content[:60]}')
Full model IDs are listed at
. Each entry shows the context window, per-token pricing, and which features (tool calling, vision, JSON mode) are supported.
Two custom headers help attribute traffic in the OpenRouter activity dashboard.
sets a human-readable label. Both are optional but strongly recommended in production.
to instruct OpenRouter to try providers in order, automatically skipping any that return a rate limit or error response.

# Smart fallbacks — try providers in order, skip on rate limit / error
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='anthropic/claude-sonnet-5',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Summarise the French Revolution.'}],
    extra_body={
        'route': 'fallback',          # enable smart routing
        'models': [                   # ordered fallback list
            'anthropic/claude-sonnet-5',
            'openai/gpt-4o',
            'meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct',
        ],
    },
)
# x-or-model in the response headers tells you which model was actually used
print(response.model)
response header) tells you which model actually served the request — important for debugging and cost attribution.
Smart fallbacks are resolved server-side: your client fires one HTTP request and OpenRouter handles the retry loop internally, typically within 50–200ms of extra latency per fallback hop.
for lowest-cost routing. OpenRouter dynamically selects among providers that host the named model.

# :nitro suffix — lowest-latency available provider
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='openai/gpt-4o-mini:nitro',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Quick question: what is 7 * 8?'}],
)

# :floor suffix — lowest-cost available provider
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='openai/gpt-4o-mini:floor',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Batch classify these 1000 items...'}],
)
gives fine-grained control: explicit provider order, data-collection opt-out, and skipping providers that lack specific parameters.
ensures your prompts are not used for third-party training — important for confidential data.
skips providers that silently ignore parameters like
Streaming works identically to the standard OpenAI SDK — pass
Tool calling uses the same OpenAI function schema. OpenRouter translates to the target provider's native format when needed.
Not all 300+ models support tool calling. Check the model's feature flags at
before relying on it. Models that do not support tools return a plain text response, which can cause a silent degradation.
OpenRouter returns token usage in the standard
field. Exact per-call cost (in USD) is available via a separate

# Check usage and cost after a call
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='openai/gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Hello'}],
)
usage = response.usage
print(f'Tokens: {usage.prompt_tokens} in / {usage.completion_tokens} out')

# OpenRouter appends cost via a generation details endpoint:
# GET https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/generation?id=<response.id>
import httpx
gen = httpx.get(
    f'https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/generation?id={response.id}',
    headers={'Authorization': 'Bearer sk-or-...'},
).json()
print(f'Cost: ${gen["data"]["total_cost"]:.6f}')
shows per-model spend over time. Use it to identify expensive call patterns and optimise routing.

some models are free but heavily rate-limited (1–5 RPM). For production workloads use paid models or add credits.

Vision-capable models accept image content in the
array. OpenRouter routes the request to any provider that hosts the chosen model, translating the image format if needed.

For OpenAI models served via OpenRouter, the OpenAI SDK's
endpoint works end-to-end, returning a Pydantic (Python) or Zod (TypeScript) validated object.

from openai import OpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel

client = OpenAI(
    base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1',
    api_key='sk-or-...',
)

class Sentiment(BaseModel):
    label: str          # 'positive' | 'negative' | 'neutral'
    confidence: float   # 0.0 – 1.0
    reason: str

# Use OpenAI structured output feature (supported on OpenAI models via OpenRouter)
response = client.beta.chat.completions.parse(
    model='openai/gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[
        {'role': 'user', 'content': 'Analyse: "The new update is fantastic!"'}
    ],
    response_format=Sentiment,
)
result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.label, result.confidence)

Structured output is an OpenAI-specific feature. For other providers (Anthropic, Gemini) use tool calling to obtain structured data — pass a single tool whose parameters schema defines the expected object, then read
endpoint listing all available models with pricing, context length, and capability flags. Query it programmatically to build dynamic model selection.

# Use OpenRouter's model filter to find cheap vision models programmatically
import httpx

models_resp = httpx.get(
    'https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models',
    headers={'Authorization': 'Bearer sk-or-...'},
).json()

vision_models = [
    m for m in models_resp['data']
    if m.get('architecture', {}).get('modality') == 'multimodal'
    and float(m.get('pricing', {}).get('completion', '999')) < 0.003  # < $3/M tokens
]

for m in sorted(vision_models, key=lambda x: float(x['pricing']['completion'])):
    print(m['id'], '$' + m['pricing']['completion'], 'per M output tokens')
The pricing fields are in dollars per
to get cost per token. The endpoint is public (no auth needed for the list, but add auth if querying rate limit info).
to inspect your remaining credits and rate limits before making batch calls.

# Check your remaining credits and rate limits
import httpx

limits = httpx.get(
    'https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/auth/key',
    headers={'Authorization': 'Bearer sk-or-...'},
).json()

print('Credits remaining:', limits['data']['limit_remaining'])
print('Rate limit (req/min):', limits['data']['rate_limit']['requests'])
print('Rate limit (tokens/min):', limits['data']['rate_limit']['tokens'])
Free-tier keys have a low RPM ceiling (typically 1–10 req/min per free model). Add credits to lift rate limits. Paid models have separate per-provider rate pools that OpenRouter aggregates.
Because model switching is a single string change, OpenRouter is useful for
. The async gather pattern lets you send the same prompt to multiple models simultaneously.

import asyncio
from openai import AsyncOpenAI
import time

client = AsyncOpenAI(
    base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1',
    api_key='sk-or-...',
)

async def timed_call(model: str, prompt: str) -> tuple[str, float]:
    start = time.perf_counter()
    resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': prompt}],
        max_tokens=200,
    )
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
    return model, elapsed

async def benchmark():
    models = [
        'openai/gpt-4o-mini',
        'anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5',
        'meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct',
    ]
    prompt = 'Summarise the importance of caching in distributed systems.'
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[timed_call(m, prompt) for m in models])
    for model, elapsed in sorted(results, key=lambda x: x[1]):
        print(f'{elapsed:.2f}s  {model}')

asyncio.run(benchmark())
Pair this with a quality evaluation step (human review, LLM-as-judge, or unit test assertions) to pick the best model for your task before committing to one in production.
OpenRouter provides a hosted, OpenAI-compatible gateway to 300+ models from a single API key and billing account.
string — no other code changes required. Supports streaming, tool calling, and JSON mode.
) retry providers server-side on rate limits or errors.
routes to the lowest-cost provider for a given model.
opts out of training data collection — essential for confidential use cases.
Compare with LiteLLM for self-hosted deployments where keys stay on-premise and network latency must be minimal.
